---
title: "Lecture 33: Spring Boot Configuration & Setup"
subtitle: "{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}"
author: "{{< meta instructor.name >}}"
date: "March 19, 2026"
code-annotations: below
---

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="custom-title">
[Lecture 33]{.baby-blue-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle">
[Spring Boot Configuration & Setup]{.white-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Week 9 | Unit V: Spring Boot & REST Services<br>
{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}<br>
{{< meta instructor.name >}}, {{< meta instructor.title >}}
</div>

---

## {data-menu-title="Learning Objectives"}

[Learning Objectives]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Explain what the Spring Framework is and why it is used
- Describe Inversion of Control (IoC) in plain English
- Differentiate constructor injection, setter injection, and field injection
- Write a basic Spring configuration using Java annotations
- Use `ApplicationContext` to retrieve and use beans
- Recognize when Spring DI reduces tight coupling in code
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Prerequisites"}

[Prerequisites]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Before this lecture, quickly revise:

- previous lecture concepts and key terminology
- related unit outcomes and expected practical skills
- baseline Java/web flow needed for in-class examples
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Syllabus Mapping"}

[Syllabus Mapping]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Unit alignment for this lecture topic
- Exam alignment for short and long questions
- Lab/practical alignment for implementation tasks
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Agenda"}

[Agenda]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- quick revision of context
- concept explanation with examples
- implementation/demo walk-through
- debugging and exam-focused recap
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Introduction"}

[Introduction]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
This lecture builds conceptual clarity first, then connects it to practical coding and exam application.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Think About It"}

[Think About It]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
What one real classroom/lab scenario best demonstrates where this topic is useful?
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="What is Spring"}

[What is the Spring Framework?]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Spring is a **lightweight Java application framework** for building enterprise-grade applications.

Core goals:
- Remove tight coupling between classes
- Manage object creation and wiring automatically
- Provide clean structure for large applications
:::

::: {.body-text-s}
Spring offers a complete ecosystem: Core, MVC, Data, Security, Boot, Cloud.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="The Problem DI Solves"}

[Tight Coupling Problem]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1-5|7-13"}
// Without DI — tightly coupled
public class OrderService {
  private PaymentService payment = new PaymentService(); // #<1>
  private EmailService email = new EmailService();       // #<2>
}

// With DI — loosely coupled
public class OrderService {
  private PaymentService payment; // #<3>
  private EmailService email;     // #<4>
  public OrderService(PaymentService p, EmailService e) {
    this.payment = p; this.email = e;
  }
}
```
1. Hard-coded dependency — cannot swap implementation
2. Hard-coded dependency — cannot mock in tests
3. Injected from outside — any implementation works
4. Injected from outside — testable and flexible

---

## {data-menu-title="IoC Container"}

[Inversion of Control (IoC)]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
**IoC** means the framework controls object creation, not your code.

Without IoC → **you** create objects  
With IoC → **Spring** creates objects and injects dependencies

The Spring IoC container is called the **ApplicationContext**.
:::

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-height: 3.0
flowchart LR
  A[Configuration Metadata] --> B[Spring IoC Container]
  C[POJO Classes] --> B
  B --> D[Ready-to-use Application Objects]
```

---

## {data-menu-title="ApplicationContext"}

[ApplicationContext vs BeanFactory]{.slide-title}

<hr>

| Feature | BeanFactory | ApplicationContext |
|---|---|---|
| Bean initialization | Lazy (on demand) | Eager at startup |
| Event publishing | No | Yes |
| i18n support | No | Yes |
| Annotation support | Limited | Full |
| Recommended for | Unit testing | All applications |

::: {.body-text-s}
In practice, always use `ApplicationContext` — `BeanFactory` is rarely used directly.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Constructor Injection"}

[Constructor Injection]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|3-4|6-10"}
@Component
public class OrderService {
  private final PaymentService paymentService; // #<1>
  private final EmailService emailService;

  @Autowired                                   // #<2>
  public OrderService(PaymentService p, EmailService e) {
    this.paymentService = p;
    this.emailService   = e;
  }
}
```
1. `final` fields — guarantees immutability after creation
2. `@Autowired` tells Spring to inject matching beans here

::: {.body-text-s}
Constructor injection is preferred — makes dependencies explicit and supports immutability.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Setter Injection"}

[Setter Injection]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|3-6"}
@Component
public class ReportService {
  private DataSource dataSource;

  @Autowired
  public void setDataSource(DataSource ds) { // #<1>
    this.dataSource = ds;
  }
}
```
1. Spring calls this setter with the matching bean after construction

::: {.body-text-m}
Use setter injection for **optional** dependencies or when circular dependency exists.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Field Injection"}

[Field Injection (and Why to Avoid It)]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1-2"}
@Component
public class UserService {
  @Autowired
  private UserRepository repo; // #<1>
}
```
1. Spring injects directly into the field — no constructor or setter needed

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
**Appears simple but has problems:**

- Cannot make fields `final`
- Hard to test without Spring container
- Hides dependencies (not visible from outside)

**Prefer constructor injection in production code.**
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Spring Config Styles"}

[Three Configuration Styles]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Spring supports three ways to define beans:
:::

| Style | How | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| XML | `<bean id="..." class="..."/>` | Legacy projects |
| Java Config | `@Configuration` + `@Bean` | Full control over creation |
| Annotation | `@Component` + `@Autowired` | Most modern projects |

::: {.body-text-s}
All three styles can coexist in the same project.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Java Config Example"}

[Java Configuration Example]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|3-6|8-13"}
@Configuration                              // #<1>
public class AppConfig {

  @Bean                                     // #<2>
  public UserRepository userRepository() {
    return new UserRepositoryImpl();
  }

  @Bean
  public UserService userService() {
    return new UserService(userRepository()); // #<3>
  }
}
```
1. Marks this class as Spring configuration source
2. Method return value is registered as a Spring bean
3. Spring calls this for the injected dependency

---

## {data-menu-title="Annotation Config Example"}

[Annotation-Based Configuration]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|7|9|11-13"}
@SpringBootApplication                      // #<1>
public class App {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    SpringApplication.run(App.class, args);
  }
}

@Repository                                 // #<2>
public class UserRepositoryImpl implements UserRepository { ... }

@Service                                    // #<3>
public class UserService {
  @Autowired UserRepository repo;
}
```
1. Component scan + auto-configuration enabled
2. Spring detects and registers this as a bean
3. Registers as service bean; repo injected automatically

---

## {data-menu-title="Using ApplicationContext"}

[Retrieving Beans from ApplicationContext]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1-2|4-5|7-8"}
// XML-based context
ApplicationContext ctx =
  new ClassPathXmlApplicationContext("beans.xml");

// Java config context
ApplicationContext ctx =
  new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext(AppConfig.class);

// Retrieve bean
UserService svc = ctx.getBean(UserService.class); // #<1>
svc.processOrder();
```
1. Spring returns the fully-wired bean instance

---

## {data-menu-title="Common Mistakes"}

[Common Mistakes to Avoid]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Using `new` to create beans instead of letting Spring manage them
- Mixing field injection everywhere instead of constructor injection
- Forgetting `@Component` or `@Bean` so Spring cannot find the class
- Using `BeanFactory` directly when `ApplicationContext` is available
- Circular dependency due to two beans injecting each other via constructors
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Exam Focus"}

[AKTU Exam-Oriented Checklist]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Prepare these high-probability questions:

1. Define Spring Framework and list its core modules
2. Explain IoC with a diagram
3. Differentiate constructor injection, setter injection, and field injection
4. Compare `ApplicationContext` and `BeanFactory`
5. Write a simple Spring Java configuration example
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Summary"}

[Summary]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
You now understand:

- Spring as an IoC container managing object lifecycle
- How dependency injection decouples components
- Three injection styles and when to use each
- Three configuration approaches (XML, Java, Annotation)
- How to retrieve and use beans from `ApplicationContext`
:::

## Memory Booster {data-menu-title="Memory Booster"}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Quick memory anchor for this lecture:

- define the core concept in one line
- map where it is used in architecture
- recall one implementation or exam-style example
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Live Demo"}

[Live Demo]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Demo focus for this lecture:

- walk through one practical code flow
- identify key configuration/code lines
- verify output and common failure points
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Resources"}

[Resources & References]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Official Spring/JSP/Servlet documentation relevant to this lecture
- Classroom notes and examples discussed in slides
- Course repository examples and demo references
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Debug"}

[Structured Debug Checklist]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
1. verify imports, annotations/tags, and object names are correct
2. check request flow, mappings, and lifecycle/state assumptions
3. inspect runtime logs and stack trace for first failing point
4. validate expected output against actual response/view
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Exam Questions"}

[Exam Preparation Questions: Short]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Define the core concept covered in this lecture.
- Differentiate related terms/approaches with one example.
- List important components, annotations, or lifecycle steps.
- Mention common mistakes and how to avoid them.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Long Questions"}

[Exam Preparation Questions: Long]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Explain the lecture topic with architecture/flow diagram and examples.
- Compare alternatives and justify best-practice usage.
- Discuss practical implementation steps and debugging strategy.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Practice"}

[Practice Task]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- implement one minimal, working example based on this lecture
- test with valid and invalid inputs
- document two errors encountered and how you fixed them
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Checklist"}

[Checklist]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Can you:

- explain the concept clearly without notes?
- implement the basic example end-to-end?
- debug common classroom/exam mistakes?
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Next Lecture"}

[Next Lecture]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Topic: Lecture 34 continuation
- Preparation required: revise this lecture summary and practice task
:::

---
## {#end-slide data-menu-title="End Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center">
<div class="custom-subtitle">
[Spring Boot Configuration & Setup]{.white-text}
</div>
<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Next: Lecture 34 - Design Patterns: Factory & Strategy
</div>
</div>
